variables.content:
The 2D calculations assume an ideal strictly two-dimensional electron gas with the bare Coulomb interaction $v_{q}=2\pi e^{2}/q$ and therefore omit finite quasi-2D layer-width form-factor corrections that, for a realistic quasi-2D quantum well or heterostructure, modify the effective interaction at large wavevectors $q$. The explicitly stated quantitative effect of such finite-width form factors is to reduce the 2D many-body renormalization by factors on the order of roughly 1.2–2 depending on carrier density and confinement details; the weak point is the assumption that omitting these finite-width form factors does not change the qualitative conclusions about the spin-polarization dependence of the effective mass (majority mass decreasing with $\zeta$, minority mass nonmonotonic).
      [14]

When comparing the experimental band-gap renormalization measured at bath temperature $T_{b}=77\ \mathrm{K}$ in real SQW samples that include some disorder with theoretical predictions for an ideal, strictly two-dimensional electron-hole plasma computed at zero temperature ($T=0$) within the RPA, the effects of finite temperature and sample disorder on the magnitude of $\Delta E_g$ are small relative to the observed discrepancy between experiment and the ideal-2D theory; therefore finite temperature and disorder are not the dominant causes of the experimentally observed reduction of band-gap renormalization, which is instead attributed mainly to finite quantum-well width and associated changes in excitonic and wave-function properties.

For the ideal (strictly two-dimensional, zero-width, disorder-free) paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass remains close to unity across the studied density parameters r_s=1, 5, and 10, and the high-precision DMC extrapolations reported here show no evidence for a strong enhancement of m^* at low density in this ideal system.

For a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) whose dispersion is described by the Rashba parabolic form $E(\mathbf{k}_{\parallel})=E_{0}+\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m^{*}}\bigl(k_{\parallel}\pm\Delta k_{\parallel}\bigr)^{2}$, if the Fermi energy $E_{\mathrm{F}}$ lies between the energy maxima at $\pm\Delta k_{\parallel}$ and the band crossing at $k_{\parallel}=0$, then the angle-integrated electronic density of states near $E_{\mathrm{F}}$ acquires a quasi-one-dimensional character (i.e., a stronger singularity or enhancement compared to the constant density of states of an ideal 2D free-electron gas) as predicted by prior theoretical analyses; this proposition assumes that the experimental band curvature and effective two-dimensionality are sufficient to produce the predicted density-of-states behaviour and that disorder, finite-temperature broadening, and contributions from other bands do not wash out the quasi-one-dimensional features.

The quasiparticle effective mass $m^{*}$ extracted from the pole of the RPA interacting Green's function depends on the interparticle-separation parameter $r_{s}$ and on the finite quantum-well width (well thickness): (i) finite quasi-two-dimensional well width reduces the Coulomb-driven mass renormalization relative to the strict two-dimensional limit because the finite thickness diminishes the Coulomb matrix element; (ii) for GaAs weak Fröhlich coupling ($\alpha\approx0.07$) the inclusion of dynamical electron–phonon interaction produces only a small correction to the uncoupled (electron–electron only) mass renormalization, and at low densities ($r_{s}>1.4$) the electron–phonon coupling further screens electron–electron interactions thereby reducing the net mass renormalization; (iii) modeling LO phonons by the bulk three-dimensional (3D) phonon approximation—i.e., treating LO phonons as unperturbed by the quantum well—reproduces the more elaborate slab-phonon-model results for wide wells (thickness greater than approximately $100\ \text{\AA}$), whereas for well widths below about $100\ \text{\AA}$ the 3D-phonon approximation overestimates the phonon contribution to mass renormalization and the discrepancy increases as the well narrows; and (iv) except for extremely narrow wells (thinner than approximately $20\ \text{\AA}$) the difference between strict-2D and quasi-2D uncoupled results can exceed the phonon-induced corrections.

The numerical observation that for the two computed electron-density parameters $r_{s}=8.7$ and $r_{s}=19.7$ with harmonic layer width $b=33\,$nm the self-consistent static local-field correction $G(q;T)$ for the finite-width quasi-two-dimensional electron gas is smaller than the $G(q;T)$ for the ideal zero-width two-dimensional model across the plotted wave-vector range implies, within the scope of these computations, that the reduction in $G(q;T)$ due to finite layer width occurs irrespective of $r_{s}$ for the two $r_{s}$ values sampled.

The quantitative crystallization threshold r_s ≳ 31, obtained from quantum Monte Carlo studies of the ideal homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas (2D HEG) in vacuum, is applied without modification to electrons localized at the surface of an insulating substrate in the heterostructure; here r_s is defined by r_s = 1/[√(π n_sub) a_B^*] with the effective Bohr radius a_B^* = 4π ε_0 ε_r ℏ^2/(μ e^2) (μ the appropriate effective mass for the substrate carriers), and this application neglects possible shifts of the crystallization threshold arising from substrate band-structure nonparabolicity, multi-valley degeneracy, reduced dimensionality of the surface, dielectric screening by proximate RMG layers, disorder, and finite temperature.
      [60]

Using the same vertical tunnelling spectrometer and measuring tunnelling from the laterally wide parasitic injection region (which behaves as an effectively two-dimensional electron gas), the interaction-renormalised effective mass of electrons in the two-dimensional parasitic regions is
      $$
      m_{2\mathrm{D}}^{*}=(0.062\pm0.002)m_{e},
      $$
      where $m_{2\mathrm{D}}^{*}$ denotes the 2D effective mass; this value is consistent with independent determinations in the literature and approaches the nominal bulk GaAs low-density mass $m_{3\mathrm{D}}^{*}=0.067m_{e}$ after modest interaction-induced renormalisation is accounted for.

Let $\bar{n}$ denote the three-dimensional uniform positive background density of a jellium slab of width $L$, so that the two-dimensional particle number per unit area is $n^{2D}=\bar{n}L$. Keeping $n^{2D}$ fixed while taking the limit $L\to 0$ yields a system whose electronic distribution is effectively constrained to the $z=0$ plane and whose in-plane electronic density equals $n^{2D}$; this limiting procedure corresponds to the strict two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) at density $n^{2D}$ and is the quasi-two-dimensional transition protocol used to compare slab results to 2D reference values.

In a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) with areal electron density $n$, let $m^{*}$ denote the effective electron mass (including interaction renormalization), let $h$ denote Planck's constant and $\hbar=h/(2\pi)$ the reduced Planck constant, let $g_{v}$ denote the valley degeneracy (take $g_{v}=2$ for (100) silicon inversion layers), and let $g_{s}$ denote the spin degeneracy (take $g_{s}=2$ in the absence of full spin polarization). The proposition is that the Fermi energy $E_{F}$ of the 2DEG is related to the density and effective mass by the semiclassical two-dimensional expression
      $$
      \frac{E_{F}}{\hbar}=\frac{n h}{g_{v} g_{s} m^{*}},
      $$
      equivalently $E_{F}=\frac{\pi\hbar^{2}n}{(g_{v}g_{s}m^{*}/2)}$, which uses the quasiparticle effective mass $m^{*}$ to account for interaction renormalization of the single-particle dispersion.
